scenario: Helix Logistics tracks 15000 shipments in Camunda 8.8 and stores routing flags in a nested context. A FEEL expression in a DMN rule must add the nested entry y.z with value 2 to the context {x:1} in a single call, producing exactly {x:1, y:{z:2}} for the downstream decision input.
question: Which FEEL call adds the nested entry y.z with value 2 to {x:1} and returns {x:1, y:{z:2}}?

options.text: Call context put({x:1}, "y.z", 2) and pass the path as a single dotted string with the new value as a number
Call context put({x:1}, ["y"], 2) and pass a single-key list with the new value as a number
Call context put({x:1}, ["y", "z"], 2) and pass the path as a list of strings with the new value as a number
Call context put({x:1}, [], 2) and pass an empty keys list with the new value as a number

a.text: Incorrect. The keys overload of context put types its second argument as list<string>; passing the literal string "y.z" is treated as a single key named y.z rather than a nested path of two keys.

b.text: Incorrect. With keys ["y"] and value 2, the documented result is {x:1, y:2}; the call sets the top-level entry y to 2 and does not create the nested y.z entry that the requirement demands.

c.text: Correct. The docs show context put({x:1}, ["y", "z"], 2) returns {x:1, y:{z:2}}; with keys [k1, k2] the function adds the nested entry k1.k2 to the context and creates intermediate contexts as needed.

d.text: Incorrect. The docs state that when keys are empty, context put returns null; no entry is added to the context, so the required {x:1, y:{z:2}} result cannot be produced this way.

explanation: context put(context, keys, value) sets a nested entry at the path defined by the keys list of strings and creates intermediate contexts as needed; with keys ["y", "z"] and value 2 it returns {x:1, y:{z:2}}, while empty keys yields null.
